markdownRemark.excerpt: I care a lot about my Spotify playlists. The titles are long and seemingly meaningless to anyone who is not in my head. I order the songs so that it works with…
markdownRemark.html:
I care a lot about my Spotify playlists. The titles are long and seemingly meaningless to anyone who is not in my head. I order the songs so that it works with or without shuffle. If a friend has a party the first thing I say is “can I make the playlist?”


I was talking with some friends on Wednesday about how we consume music today. Some of us look for the most obscure live recordings on Youtube, another prioritizes supporting musicians so uses Bandcamp, and another is always on the lookout for the next up-and-comer on SoundCloud. All of us agreed that our favourite way of exploring new music is through friends or serendipity.

My favourite thing is when I wake up to 5 different Spotify links from my friends. My favourite thing is being somewhere and having to Shazam a song — that’s serendipity. What is absolutely not my favourite is the AI curated mixes that pounce on me the moment I open Spotify. Here is an example.



There is no short-selling the potential and possibilities of AI. Language and image processing models have seen exponential improvement over the past 5 years when prior the industry had been stuck on models with theoretical underpinnings in the ’80s. I do however think the computer should stick to what it knows best and art should be left for those of us with a heart.

frontmatter.title: The Beauty of Curation
frontmatter.description: Modern day curation has been handed off to the algorithm and it's offensive that people think a computer has better taste than me.

frontmatter.title: On Not Being Afraid of Tech Debt
